details.name: Theorem 1 (Taylor's Theorem)
details.description: If $f(z)$ is analytic in a region $\Omega$ containing $a$, one has $$f(z) = f(a) + \frac{f'(a)}{1!}(z-a) + \cdots + \frac{f^{(n-1)}(a)}{(n-1)!}(z-a)^{n-1} + f_{n}(z)(z-a)^{n},$$ where $f_{n}(z)$ is analytic in $\Omega$. Moreover, if $C$ is the boundary of a closed disk contained in $\Omega$ with center $a$, then $f_{n}(z)$ has the representation $$f_{n}(z) = \frac{1}{2\pi i} \int_{C} \frac{f(\zeta)\, d\zeta}{(\zeta - a)^{n}(\zeta - z)} \quad (z \text{ inside } C).$$

The Lean theorem states exactly this: given two circles A, B that are nonintersecting (disjoint as sets), there exists a Möbius transformation T and circles A', B' such that T maps A to A' and B to B', and A', B' are concentric (same center). All supporting definitions are genuine and mathematically correct: Circle is a standard Euclidean circle (center, positive radius), Nonintersecting is set-disjointness, Concentric means equal centers, MoebiusTransformation correctly encodes (az+b)/(cz+d) with ad-bc≠0 (the book's 'linear transformation' in complex analysis), and MapsCircle captures both forward mapping and surjectivity. The theorem is fully proved (no sorry), the dependency chain is healthy with no alerts, and the proof follows the book's strategy (transform one circle to a line, then use inverse points). The handling of the Möbius pole (denominator zero) in ℂ rather than the Riemann sphere is the only adaptation, done correctly without weakening the result.
[proof_integrity=5/5] This is an exemplary formalization. The proof is 885 lines of substantial mathematical work, faithfully following the book's proof strategy: (1) pick a point on circle A, apply inversion to send A to a line, (2) construct a second Möbius transformation using inverse points to map the line and the image of B into concentric circles, (3) compose the transformations. Newman.differentiableAt_of_closedRightHa.explanation: This lemma states that a function differentiable on {z | Re(z) ≥ 0} is differentiable everywhere.
